coredata.dc:title: Green and cost-effective synthesis of carbon dots from date kernel and their application as a novel switchable fluorescence probe for sensitive assay of Zoledronic acid drug in human serum and cellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Amin, Niloufar
Afkhami, Abbas
Hosseinzadeh, Leila
Madrakian, Tayyebeh
coredata.dc:description: A new label-free, sensitive and selective off and on signaling fluorescence platform for assay of trace levels of Zoledronic acid (ZA) drug in human biological samples based on nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) - ferric ions (Fe3+) was designed. The fluorescence probe, N-CDs, was synthesized for the first time through a facile, eco-friendly and one-step hydrothermal treatment using date kernel as the precursor without any need to use chemical reagents. These CDs exhibited excellent water solubility, ionic and photo stability in various circumstances and a highly relative quantum yield of 12.5%. In the presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity (FL) for N-CDs was strongly quenched due to the interaction between ferric ions and the functional groups at the N-CDs (switch off). Afterwards, by the addition of ZA, the fluorescence sensor status turned to “ON” (switch on) due to the dominance of ZA in the competition between functional groups on the surface of N-CDs and phosphate groups in ZA in the interaction with Fe3+ results in removing Fe3+ from the surface of N-CDs. Under the optimized conditions, the proposed fluorescence probe (N-CDs-Fe3+) exhibited good sensing performance for ZA assay with a linearity from 0.1 μM to 10.0 μM, a detection limit of 0.04 μM and the precision of 2.70%. The developed N-CDs-Fe3+ sensor was successfully used for the assay of ZA contents with good recoveries and selectivity in human serum samples. Meanwhile, the in vitro cytotoxic activity and cellular uptake of N-CDs were investigated on human osteosarcoma (MG-63) cell line.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Date kernel
Switchable fluorescence sensor
Zoledronic acid drug
Human osteosarcoma cell line
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for fluorescence detection of ZA
Synthesis and structural investigation of date kernel -derived N-CDs
Optical and spectral investigations of as-prepared N-CDs
Design principles of the turn “off –on” nanosensor and mechanism investigation
Application of N-CDs-Fe3+ to biological sample analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV-vis absorption spectra of the synthesized N-CDs (blue line). Fluorescence maximum excitation (red line) and emission spectra (green line) of the N-CDs (λex = 340 nm, λem = 430 nm). Inset: Photograph of N-CDs under illumination of daylight (right) and UV (365 nm) light (left). (B) Fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs in aqueous solution at different excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 400 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence quenching of N-CDs towards different cations with a final concentration of 500.0 μM (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs in the absence and in the presence of various individual metal ions). (B) FL spectra of N-CDs solution at different conditions. (a) represents N-CDs solution alone, (b) represents N-CDs solution containing ZA, (c) represents N-CDs containing Fe3+, (d) N-CDs solution containing Fe3+ and ZA, (e) represents mixing solution of Fe3+ and ZA. Concentration of the N-CDs, Fe3+ and ZA were 0.1 mg mL−1 and 50.0 μM.

Effect of (A) pH, (B) dosage of N-CDs and (C) reaction time on the fluorescent detection of ZA. (F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs- Fe3+ system in the presence and absence of ZA, respectively).

(A) Fluorescent emission spectra of the N-CDs aqueous solutions upon the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–500 μM). (B) Stern–Volmer plots (F0/F) for the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs by Fe3+ ions in the range of 0.5–250 μM. (C) FL spectra of the N-CDs-Fe3+ system upon the subsequent addition of ZA (0–100 μM). (D) Fluorescence recovery efficiency (F-F0) response of the N-CDs - Fe3+ system toward ZA. (The inset: linear relationship between F-F0 and the concentrations of ZA (0.1–10.0 μM) at a concentration level of 0.1 mg mL−1 (N-CDs), 50 μM (Fe3+ solution), the incubation time was 25.0 min before detection in 10 mM acetate buffer of pH 4.0.

Selectivity of the proposed method for ZA detection. (A) Effect of different biological molecules (ZA, FLD drug, Acetate, Phenylalanine, Ascorbic acid, Uric acid, L-Cystein, Glutathione, BSA, Na+, Ca2+) on the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs-Fe3+ system (F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs-Fe3+ without and with biological molecules, respectively). (B) Effect of a series 1 mM of biological molecules (Glucose, Lactose, Urea, Ascorbic acid, Uric acid, L-Cystein, Glutathione, BSA, Na+, Ca2+) on the selectivity of the N-CDs-Fe3+ system in the presence of 10 μM ZA. At 0.1 mg/mL N-CDs, 50 μM Fe3+ solution, 10 mM acetate buffer pH 4.0.

Fluorescence microscopic images of MG-63 cells treated with 20.0 μg mL-1 N-CDs at 37 °C for 25 min (panels in (a) row), MG-63 cells treated with N-CDs-Fe3+(50 μM) at 37 °C for 25 min (panels in (b) row), MG-63 cells treated with the N-CDs- Fe3+-ZA using different concentrations of ZA (10 μM, 30 μM, 60 μM and 100 μM) at 37 °C for 25 min (panels in (c) row). Scale bar is 100 μm. Cell images recorded on λex = 340 nm.
Illustration of synthesis of N-CDs, fluorescence sensing strategy for Zoledronic acid detection based on N-CDs and application to cell imaging.
Assay results of Zoledronic acid in human serum sample.

head.ce:title: Green and cost-effective synthesis of carbon dots from date kernel and their application as a novel switchable fluorescence probe for sensitive assay of Zoledronic acid drug in human serum and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Bu-Ali Sina University, Hamedan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Sciences Research Center, School of Pharmacy, Kermanshah University of Medical Sciences, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new label-free, sensitive and selective off and on signaling fluorescence platform for assay of trace levels of Zoledronic acid (ZA) drug in human biological samples based on nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) - ferric ions (Fe3+) was designed. The fluorescence probe, N-CDs, was synthesized for the first time through a facile, eco-friendly and one-step hydrothermal treatment using date kernel as the precursor without any need to use chemical reagents. These CDs exhibited excellent water solubility, ionic and photo stability in various circumstances and a highly relative quantum yield of 12.5%. In the presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity (FL) for N-CDs was strongly quenched due to the interaction between ferric ions and the functional groups at the N-CDs (switch off). Afterwards, by the addition of ZA, the fluorescence sensor status turned to “ON” (switch on) due to the dominance of ZA in the competition between functional groups on the surface of N-CDs and phosphate groups in ZA in the interaction with Fe3+ results in removing Fe3+ from the surface of N-CDs. Under the optimized conditions, the proposed fluorescence probe (N-CDs-Fe3+) exhibited good sensing performance for ZA assay with a linearity from 0.1 μM to 10.0 μM, a detection limit of 0.04 μM and the precision of 2.70%. The developed N-CDs-Fe3+ sensor was successfully used for the assay of ZA contents with good recoveries and selectivity in human serum samples. Meanwhile, the in vitro cytotoxic activity and cellular uptake of N-CDs were investigated on human osteosarcoma (MG-63) cell line.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, statistics and evidence, in clinical studies of cancer patients, reveal that development of multiple myeloma and bone metastases are prevalent, particularly between malignant breast, prostate and lung cancers [1,2]. In these patients, severe effects of metastatic bone tumors are associated with bone resorption, produce by means of osteoclast cells, which result in development of malignant hypercalcaemia and pathological fractures due to increased fragility [3,4].

Bisphosphonates (BPs) are synthetic analogs of pyrophosphate which bind with high affinity to the bone matrix [5]. These compounds, interrupting bone resorption and then reducing osteoclast-induced bone loss [6], have the potential to be used for the treatment of bone diseases such as osteoporosis [7], Paget disease [8], fibrous dysplasia [9] and primary metastatic bone tumors [10]. Zoledronic acid (ZA) is a heterocyclic imidazole third-generation bisphosphonate which induces apoptosis of osteoclast cells through inhibition of farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase, a key enzyme in the prenylation of proteins [11,12], that regulates the proliferation of human tumor cells. Moreover, an immensity of preclinical studies approved that nitrogen-containing bisphosphonates, particularly ZA, exhibit direct antitumor effects primarily in human breast cancer cell lines by reducing tumor cell proliferation and then promoting their apoptosis in vitro [5,12].

Since administration of ZA drug along with very low doses to cancer patients who suffer from bone metastases is in the form of intravenous or transdermal injection and its accumulation mainly in bone mineral matrix hence to exact valuation of the pharmacokinetics and toxic effect of this drug in body fluids such as urine and plasma require a sensitive quantitative analytical method [13,14].

Typically, there are some challenges in purification and analysis of ZA in biological matrices by conventional HPLC analysis because of its high polarity nature and strong acidic property. Thus special techniques are needed to separate the analyte from biological interfering substances. So far, a number of methods for the detection of ZA have been developed, but as it is retained hardly on common chromatographic columns such as C18 or C8, the most common among them, is an ion – pairing agent coupled with different methods including ion exchange high performance liquid chromatographic (IEC) [15], ion–pair high–performance liquid chromatography [16], reverse phase liquid chromatography (RP-LC) [17], liquid chromatography coupled with tandem mass spectrometry (LC/MS/MS) [13]. Nevertheless, these methods often suffer from a variety of drawbacks, such as tedious purification of samples, sophisticated instrumentation and time consuming processes, which might limit their application in the daily route quantification of ZA in real samples. Therefore, of interest is the development of a sensitive, inexpensive, simple and safe - to-use appropriate method for detection of ZA. In order to overcome these challenges, fluorescence assay is regarded as a more favorable approach owing to its low cost, simplicity, high sensitivity and fast response [18].

As a novel member of the fluorescence carbon-nanomaterials group, carbon dots (CDs) with less than 10 nm size have attracted tremendous attention in area of bioanalytics [19] and bioimaging [20,21] owing to excellent biocompatibility, water dispersibility, easiness of functionalization, chemical inertness, tunable photoluminescence, high photostability and all of the most important low toxicity [20,22]. Generally, carbon dots can be synthesized by both top-down (laser ablation [23], electrochemical etching [19], arc discharge [22], etc.) and bottom-up (ultrasonic synthesis [24], microwave-assisted synthesis [25], hydrothermal route [26], etc.) approaches. However, these methods suffer from various drawbacks including tedious methodology, time-consuming processes, expensive starting materials and toxic chemicals [27]. Thus, to decrease the overall preparation costs and to make use of nontoxic and eco-friendly route, natural materials, as carbon sources exhibit amazing advances to the synthesize of CDs because of its green chemistry nature. On the other hand, it is of high interest to explore the green sources of CDs because these are cheap, clean, nontoxic, sustainable and easily accessible. Until now numerous biomass materials such as soy milk (2.6%) [28], grass (6.2%) [29], strawberry juice (6.3%) [30], coffee grounds (3.8%) [31], Bamboo leaves (7.1%) [32], ginger (13.4%) [33], konja flour (7%) [21], Centella asiatica (3%) [34], Green tea (4.3%) [35], milk (12%) [36] (QY for the produced CDs is given in the parenthesis), were used as the precursors to prepare N-CDs by hydrothermal treatment. Although these fluorescent CDs can be obtained from “green” raw materials, most of them were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment in organic solvent or with low QY. Therefore, it is highly desirable from point view of green chemistry and cost effective strategies for the synthesis of CDs, using a food waste that quite common and low cost in routine life.

Herein, we reported for the first time, the preparation of water dispersible N-CDs via one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of date kernel without using any pretreatment. Indeed, one of the fantastic features of our work is that, the resulted N-CDs have a higher quantum yield (12.5%) than many synthesized N-CDs from bio-mass sources and another feature is that we avoided the use of high cost or toxic raw material or addition of acids and bases that makes the procedure according to green strategy. Furthermore, a well-planned, versatile, eco-friendly environmentally, economical and simple strategy based on as-synthesized N-CDs for the determination of ZA without using any other chemical reagent was developed. To address the mentioned goal, we have constructed a novel green photoluminescence on-off-on sensor by exploiting the strong affinity of phosphate groups toward Fe3+. The sensing system was arranged through quenching the fluorescence of N-CDs by Fe3+ via electron transfer, and then the quenching effect could be inversed by coordination of Fe3+ to ZA. The extent of restoration of N-CDs- Fe3+ is directly related to the concentration of ZA. In addition in the current study for the first time, the biocompatibility of N-CDs prepared from date kernel has been checked using osteoblast-like cells MG-63 as a model system. We also explored the labeling of MG-63 cells with the carbon dots.

Metal salts such as NaCl, KCl, FeCl3·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O, CaCl2, NiCl2. 6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, Zn (NO3)3.6H2O, PbCl2, MnCl2. 4H2O, CuSO4. 5H2O, HgCl2, CdCl2, AgNO3 were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Ascorbic acid, L-Cystein, Lactose, Glucose, Urea and Phenylalanine were prepared by Sigma-Aldrich. Zoledronic acid hydrated disodium salt and Fludarabine phosphate were supplied by Faranshimi Company (Tehran, Iran). The osteoblast-like MG63 cell line was obtained from Pasteur Institute (Tehran, Iran). Quinine sulfate 98%, Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), 3-(4, 5-dimethyl-2-thiazoyl)-2, 5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT, 98%), fetal bovine serum, penicillin and streptomycin were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. (St. Louis, MO, USA). CH3COONa/CH3COOH buffer solution (0.01 M, pH 4.0) was prepared by diluting desired volume of CH3COOH and desired weight of CH3COONa in double-distilled water. All chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Double deionized water was used throughout all the experiments.

All fluorescence measurements were performed by a Perkin Elmer spectrofluorometer equipped with a Xenon arc lamp. Excitation and emission slits were set at 10 nm. The UV-Vis spectra were recorded using a diode array UV-8453 Agilent spectrophotometer (USA). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded on an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, 38066 Riva, d/G. via M. Misone, 11/D (TN) Italy). The size and morphologies were achieved by a Philips-CM30 transmission electron microscope using an acceleration voltage of 100 kV. Raman spectra were measured with a Lab RAM HORIBA JY, Edison, NJ equipped with a He-Ne 633 nm Laser. Zeta potential measurements were carried out on a Nano ZS zeta (red badge) ZEN 3600 zetasizer (Malvern, England). MTT assay was measured using a Synergy H1 ELISA plate reader (BioTek, ELx800™, USA). Fluorescent microscopic images of MG63 cells were taken on Cytation 5 cell imaging multi-mode reader (BioTek). Cyclic voltammetry measurement was performed using an Autolab potentiostat/galvanostat (Vertex, Ivium Technologies, Netherlands). Finally, a Metrohm model 713 pH-meter was used for pH measurements.

The collected date kernels were washed with water to eliminate dust particles and thoroughly dried under the sun light. They were then ground into a fine powder and used as the precursor for the synthesis of fluorescent N-CDs. An amount of 2.0 g of this powder was dispersed in 10.0 mL of deionized water under vigorous stirring using magnetic stirrer for 30 min. Then the mixture was placed into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. The resultant dark brownish suspension was filtered through a Whatman 32 mm filter paper to remove useless and insoluble particles and then centrifuged at 16000 rpm for 20 min to remove large dots, after cooling down to room temperature. In order to further purification, the transparent solution was dialyzed for 24 h against double distilled water using a dialysis membrane (Cut off = 3500 Da). A certain amount of obtained N-CDs were dispersed in water and used for further studies.

The quantum yield of the synthesized N-CDs was determined via Quinine sulfate (in 0.1 M H2SO4 with QY = 0.54) as a fluorescence reference material according to the following equation [37,38]:
(1)
Q =
Q R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
n 2
n R 2

In this equation, Q is the quantum yield, I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, A is the absorbance at the excitation wavelength and n is the refractive index of the solvent. In addition, the subscript R scores reference material with known Q. In order to minimize reabsorption effects, absorbance values in the 1.0 cm fluorescence quartz cuvette were set under 0.1 at the excitation wavelength (340 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for fluorescence detection of ZA
ce:section.ce:para: For the determination of ZA, a 50 μL of 1.0 mg mL−1 of N-CDs solution, 600 μL of acetate buffer solution (50.0 mM, pH 4.0) and 150 μL of Fe3+ solution (1.0 mM) were mixed. The mixture was diluted to 3 mL using ultrapure water and allowed to stand at room temperature for 25 min. After equilibration, a certain volume of ZA solution that was prepared by diluting a concentrated stock solution (10.0 mM) with water was introduced into the system. Subsequently, the FL of the mixture was measured after 1 min. To investigate the selectivity of Fe3+ and ZA detection, the similar procedure was carried out for the sensing of other cations and biological compounds. All the fluorescence spectra were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 340 nm and the emission wavelength of 430 nm.

The human serum samples were collected from healthy volunteers with informed consent and treated according to the general procedure [39] as follow. Initially, different concentrations of ZA were introduced into 0.5 mL ZA free serum sample followed by 0.4 mL acetonitrile in order to separate serum protein. After that, the mixture was subjected to centrifugation for 15 min, 3500 rpm. Finally, the supernatant was collected and used for the detection of ZA as described above.

For in vitro bioimaging, MG-63 cells were grown on DMEM into each 35 mm glass culture dishes in accordance with the amounts as noted above. Then, these cells were washed three times with phosphate buffer solution of pH 7.4 and the medium was replaced with 20.0 μg mL-1 N-CDs solution. After 25 min incubation at 37 °C and again washing with phosphate buffer solution of pH 7.4 the cell images was acquired by fluorescence microscope at excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Meanwhile, mentioned general trend for 20.0 μg mL-1 N-CDs solution alone, 20.0 μg mL-1 N-CDs - 50.0 μM Fe3+ and 20.0 μg mL-1 N-CDs - 50.0 μM Fe3+ - different concentrations of ZA treated with MG-63 cells are totally similar.

The MG63 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM-F12) with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum, 100 U mL−1 penicillin, and 100 mg mL−1 streptomycin. The medium was changed 2–3 days and sub-cultured when the cell population density reached to 70–80% confluence. In order to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the as-prepared N-CDs, MTT assay was performed on MG-63 cells. Firstly, the cells were cultured onto a 96-well cell culture plates at the density of 15000 cells/well. After 24 h, various concentrations of N-CDs were added to the wells, followed by incubation for 24 h. Subsequently, 20 μL of 5.0 mg mL MTT solution was put into each well and the cells incubated for an additional 4 h. Then, the medium was removed, and the formazan crystals were dissolved in DMSO (200 μL well−1). Absorbance was determined on an ELISA plate reader with a test wavelength of 570 nm and a reference wavelength of 630 nm to obtain sample signal (OD570–OD630).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and structural investigation of date kernel -derived N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since date kernel contains a significant amount of carbohydrate (78.3 g/100 g date kernels), Fat (10.5 g/100 g), as a carbon skeletal, in addition to lesser amounts of protein (5.7 g/100 g) and Ash (1.05 g/100 g) suggesting potential as a discarded carbon source for making nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) [40,41]. Therefore, we selected aqueous hydrothermal treatment for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from date kernel because of the formation process contains dehydration, polymerization and carbonization of carbohydrates and then passivation [42]. In order to achieve the appropriate quantum yield and electronic properties, synthesis conditions including temperature, time and mass ratios of date kernel to water were optimized (Table S1). Finally, 200 °C and 8 h were chosen for optimal reaction temperature and time.

Representative TEM images for the prepared N-CDs along with histogram of their sizes are provided in Fig. S1A. From the TEM image can be inferred well that the N-CDs have a uniform dispersion and narrow size distribution between 1 and 5 nm with an average diameter of 2.5 nm. The XRD pattern of as-prepared N-CDs (Fig. S1B) showed a strong broad diffraction peak centered at 2θ = 21.5° and a weak peak at 2θ = 42.5° which is similar to the previously reported works [43,44]. This finding confirms that the carbon dots are amorphous in nature and do not possess other crystalline phases. Raman spectroscopy was conducted for the confirmation of structural properties of CDs. As shown in Fig. S1C, there are mainly two peaks at 1275.96 cm−1 and 1619.26 cm− 1 corresponding to D-band and G-band, respectively. The D band is in line with edge distortion and structural defects and the G band is related to the vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a structure. The relative intensity of the D band and G band (ID/IG) is 0.78 implies that N-CDs were composed of graphitic defects sp2 carbon atom in amorphous carbon which agrees well with the result from XRD analysis [21].

The surface structure and composition of the as-prepared C-dots were then investigated using FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S1D, the strong absorption band around 3200-3400 cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of N-H and O-H. The sharp peak at 1769 cm−1 and 1594 cm−1 assigned to C=O and C=C, respectively. Also two bands at 1048 cm−1 and 1072 cm−1 are related to C-O bending vibration. In addition, the absorption peaks at 1666 cm−1, 1420 cm−1 and 2929 cm−1 correspond to the N–H bending vibrations, bending vibration of C-N deformation and C–H stretching vibrations, respectively [45]. As well as, elemental analysis (EDX) (Fig. S2) is tabulated in Table S2 and manifest that the N-CDs are composed of C (45.96%), N (5.63%), O (44.39%) and S (0.24%) atoms. Therefore carbon content is higher in the carbon dots than date kernel, indicating carbonization during hydrothermal treatment. Zeta potential of the N-CDs is −10.6 mV, indicating there are lots of negative charged groups on the surface of N-CDs.

On the basis of the above characterization findings, the hydrothermal carbonization of the date kernel can result in N-CDs and there are plentiful of the hydroxyl, carboxylic and amine groups on the surface of carbon dots so that their well dispensability in water and high affinity to attachment of biomolecules without any chemical modification due to the existence of these functional groups [44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and spectral investigations of as-prepared N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the N-CDs were characterized by the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy, displayed in Fig. 1 . As shown in Fig. 1A, the N-CDs dispersed in water exhibit the absorption peak at 275 nm similar to that of reported fluorescence CDs, which could be assigned to π-π* transition of aromatic C=C bond [25,46]. Notably, the strong blue fluorescence of N-CDs excited by a 365 nm UV lamp can be seen clearly by the naked eye (inset in Fig. 1A). Fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of the N-CDs with the maximum values around 340 nm and 430 nm, respectively, are given in Fig. 1A. Moreover, with the increase of the excitation wavelength from 360 to 480 nm, the photoluminescence peak red-shifts from 452 nm (blue emission) to 525 nm which illustrating an excitation – dependent emission of N-CDs (Fig. 1B). Such behavior is one of the most prominent features of carbon-dots may be due to variation of particle sizes and the distribution of surface energy traps of the carbon dots [46].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design principles of the turn “off –on” nanosensor and mechanism investigation
ce:section.ce:para: To distinguish selective fluorescence sensing of N-CDs towards metal ions, the quenching performance was carried out in the presence of 500.0 μM different metal ions. As revealed from Fig. 2 A, only Fe3+ showed a quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs probe among the others tested cations as far as more than 92% of the fluorescence of N-CDs was extinguished by the addition of 500.0 μM Fe3+ ions. Such a specific fluorescence quenching effect may be due to that Fe3+ ion coordinates very strongly with phenolic hydroxyl, carboxylic and amine groups at the surface of N-CDs. This causes that excited electrons belongs to of N-CDs instead of returning to its ground state and generation of fluorescence, transfer to the unfilled 3d orbitals of Fe3+ and resulting in FL quenching because of the nonradiative electron transfer [47]. It should be mentioned that, the probable reason for the high affinity of the as-prepared N-CDs to Fe3+ over the other metal ions is that the energy value of lone pair orbital belongs to Fe3+ ion lies between LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) and HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) related to the carbon dots therefore the as-prepared N-CDs act as an oxidative PET (photoinduced electron transfer).

The HOMO and LUMO levels (E HOMO and E LUMO) were estimated from the oxidation potential according to empirical equations [48,49]:
(2)
E
H O M O
= − e
(
E
o x
+ 44 )
(3)
E
L U M O
= − e
(
E
r e d
+ 44 )
where Eox and Ered are the oxidation and reduction potentials for N-CDs, respectively. The Ered was determined to be −0.59 eV (Fig. S4). So the corresponding ELUMO was calculated to be −3.81 eV. Unfortunately, the oxidation behaviors were irreversible; therefore, we were unable to accurately estimate the HOMO energy level. To determine the HOMO levels, we combined the CV reduction potential with the energy band gap (Eg) resulting from the absorption edge in the absorption spectrum of N-CDs [49].
(4)
E
H O M O
=
E
L U M O
−
E g

Eg was estimated to be 3.29 eV. So, the EHOMO of CQDs is calculated to be −7.1 eV. According to literature results and previous studies on complexation between Fe3+ and phenolic hydroxyl of N-CDs, the crystal field stabilization energy (CFSE) of the complex was estimated 2.70 eV [50].

It can be clearly observed in results, the electron transfer in the excited state of N-CDs can occur from the LUMO of N-CDs to the LUMO of Fe3+ indicating that electron transfer between N-CDs and Fe3+ is possible and N-CDs can acts as an oxidative PET. Thus, such efficient electron transfer provides appreciable quenching of the fluorescence by Fe3+ [50,51].

For a better understanding of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs, UV-Vis absorption study was performed. It can be clearly observed in Fig. S5 that the absorption around 220 nm changes negligible but the typical absorption peak at 275 nm gently red-shifts to 345 nm accompanied by absorbance enhancement with the increase of Fe3+ concentration, which indicated that Fe3+ could interact with N-CDs to affect the charge transfer process on N-CDs [52].

As already discussed in the literature [53–55], Fe3+ ion has a strong binding affinity to phosphate groups or multiphosphate ligands and complex formation occurs. Inspired by this, we considered that ZA with special two phosphate groups would synergistically coordinate with Fe3+ through Fe-O-P bond and owing to their specifically interaction, bound Fe3+ ions released from the surface of carbon dots to bind with ZA upon its introduction. Thus, as anticipated, after subsequent addition of ZA fluorescence is seen to be significantly regenerated. The principle of the switchable platform based on a competitive behavior occurring between ZA and Fe3+ releases from the N-CDs- Fe3+ complex was shown in Scheme 1 .

To investigate the sensing ability of ZA, fluorescence spectra of the N-CDs under different conditions was studied. As can be seen in Fig. 2B, ignorable fluorescence change was observed in the present of ZA as well as on adding ZA into the solution of Fe3+ no traceable fluorescence was observed thus ZA could not quench the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs without Fe3+. Additionally, once ZA was added into the N-CDs-Fe3+ system, the formation of complex between ZA and Fe3+ ions occurred and fluorescence of N-CDs becomes ON.

In order to characterize the surface charge of the N-CDs as well as to confirm the predicted mechanism, Zeta (ζ) potential measurements were carried out. The obtained zeta potential of as-synthesized N-CDs was found to be −10.6 mV. The negatively charged surface could be ascribed to the presence of surface groups such as –COOH, –OH and –NH2 that spread throughout the surface. As shown in Fig. S6, the ζ-potential of the carbon dots shifted from −10.6–6.58 mV in the presence of Fe3+ because of the complex (non-fluorescence ground-state static complex) formation between the positively charged Fe3+ ions and negatively charged N-CDs which is the result of electrostatic attraction. Interestingly, after addition of ZA to the N-CDs-Fe3+ system, a decrease in ζ-potential (1.90) was observed (Fig. S6C). This decrease in charge can be due to break up the aforementioned complex because of the development of a new complex between Fe3+ and ZA which drastically ZA removes Fe3+ from the surface of N-CDs. The as performed study clearly supports our claims about proposed mechanism.

In order to improve the sensitivity of the fluorescence nanosensor, some experimental conditions, including pH, dosage of N-CDs and reaction time were optimized using one-factor-at-a-time method. Firstly, the influence of pH on the sensing system was investigated in the range 3.0–9.0 and demonstrated in Fig. 3 A. The FL intensity of N-CDs quenched on the addition of Fe3+ at each pH value from 3.0 to 9.0 but the best quenching effect occurred when the pH was 4.0 (acidic media). With the addition of ZA, the FL intensity increased significantly just at pH 4.0 and the largest ratio of F/F0 (F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs-Fe3+ system in the presence and absence of ZA, respectively) was seen at this pH. These observations can be described as follows: The hydrated Fe3+ cation is stable under acidic conditions, but under alkaline pH, the Fe3+ reacts with OH− ion to form Fe(OH)3 which this behavior interestingly consistent with the reported works [56]. .As a result, the quenching efficiency of Fe3+ would be sharply decreased under alkaline condition. Besides, literature suggests that strong affinity of the Fe3+ cation and phosphate group in the pH range 3.0–5.0 is related to the ionization of the second acidic oxygen of phosphate [57] and at higher pH 5 because of competition between phosphate group and hydroxide ions to Fe3+ and overcoming hydroxide ions, this affinity and consequently recovery effect decreased. As a result, acetate buffer of pH 4.0 was used in the sensing platform of Fe3+ and ZA.

The optimized concentration of N-CDs was selected by evaluation the fluorescence recovery of N-CDs-Fe3+ system in the presence of ZA at pH 4.0. The results inferred that at low concentrations of the FL probe (0.1 mg mL−1), the highest ratio signal is achieved (Fig. 3B). The presence of a lot of polar functional groups helps to form agglomeration at high concentrations and resulted in decrease FL intensity of N-CDs.

The time-dependent fluorescence intensity of N-CDs-Fe3+ was displayed in Fig. 3C, which indicates that FL intensity of N-CDs after the addition of Fe3+ gradually quenched and remained constant, approximately one third of its original FL intensity, after 25 min. Therefore, 25 min was selected as the optimum reaction time for Fe3+. Meanwhile, the response of N-CDs - Fe3+- ZA system to time was very fast and stable after 1 min. A further increase of time did not lead to any detectable recovery of the fluorescence of the N-CDs. Thus the recovery process is completed within 1.0 min.

As shown in Fig. 4 A, under the optimized conditions, when the different concentrations of Fe3+ were added into the N-CDs aqueous solution with the incubation time of 25 min, the fluorescence intensity at 430 nm decreased gradually.

In order to ensure the details of this fluorescence quenching, we used the Stern–Volmer equation, which can be described as bellows:
(5)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]
where Fo and F indicate the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. [Q], denotes the concentration of Fe3+ and KSV is the Stern–Volmer constant. Fig. 4B shows the significant linearity of Stern–Volmer plot (Fo/F) against the concentration of Fe3+. The value of KSV equals slope of Stern-Volmer plot and the value of Kq equals to KSV/τ0. Kq is the bimolecular quenching rate constant and τ0 is the FL lifetime of N-CDs in the absence of Fe3+. In this study KSV was obtained as 0.0109 μM−1 and inasmuch as fluorescence life time of the most carbon dots approximately is 1.0 ns, here the resulted Kq has the value about 11.0 × 1012 M−1 S−1 which indicated that static quenching contributes to the N-CDs towards Fe3+ detection [58]. Therefore, the results given from Stern–Volmer plot were consistent with the observations of the absorption spectra as discussed in section.3.3 based on formation of non-emissive ground-state complex.

For turning back the extinguished fluorescence, ZA was introduced into the solution of N-CDs-Fe3+ complex, after 25 min incubation time for the reaction between Fe3+ and N-CDs the fluorescence gradually resumed up to 90% of its original intensity (Fig. 4C). As demonstrated in Fig. 4D the fluorescence response (F-Fo) increased gradually with increasing ZA concentration and a linear calibration in the range 0.1–10.0 μM was acquired. The limit of detection was estimated to be 0.04 μM which was calculated according to 3s/slope of the calibration curve (s: standard deviation of blank samples) [46].

In order to evaluate the performance of the proposed sensing platform, selectivity studies toward ZA were carried out. For this purpose, some cations, anions and also some biological molecules which usually exist in blood serum including Phenylalanine, Ascorbic acid, Uric acid, l-cysteine, Glutathione, Bovine serum albumin, Ca2+, SO4 2−, CH3COO− were chosen to investigate their effect on the response of the N-CDs-Fe3+ at identical conditions. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 A, significant enhancement in the fluorescence intensity were observed after the addition of 30 μM ZA, whereas no obvious alterations in the emission were detected after the addition of other molecules or ions even with ten- or hundred-folds excess over ZA. Such high selectivity is caused by the high affinity of Fe3+ for phosphate group or it is better to be said for formation of Fe-O-P. It should be noted that ZA drug due to having two phosphate groups synergistically coordinate to Fe3+. To prove this claim, the ability of ZA drug to recover the fluorescence of N-CDs-Fe3+ complexes were compared with Fludarabine (FLD) drug, which is a chemotherapy drug used in the treatment of leukemia and lymphoma and have a phosphate group in its structure. It was found that FLD drug, due to not having a synergistical effect, could not affect the FL response. Furthermore, competition experiments were performed, in which ZA and other ions or molecules were added into the N-CDs-Fe3+ solution. In this case, addition of these substances did not affect the fluorescence recovery of N-CDs-Fe3+ complex in the presence of ZA. The results are illustrated in Fig. 5B, which clearly demonstrates this new sensing platform has been successfully used for the selective detection of ZA in blood serum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of N-CDs-Fe3+ to biological sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: For further investigation of the applicability and reliability of the developed N-CDs-Fe3+ based chemosensor, the sensing of ZA in human serum was carried out. Treatments of serum sample performed according to the procedures described in experimental section in supplementary information. Afterward, spiking recovery was performed by adding a certain amount of the treated serum samples into the N-CDs-Fe3+ system followed by employing the calibration curve obtained under the same experimental conditions and then the fluorescence intensity was measured. Table 1 shows that the recoveries within 92.2–104.0% were achieved, and the relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) was less than 3.2% for 0.1 μM of ZA, which indicated the high analytical precision of the present method.

It is necessary to evaluate the biocompatibility of as-synthesis N-CDs before doing any biological application. For this purpose, cellular toxicity of the N-CDs was investigated using human MG-63 cells in a MTT assay and the results were shown in Fig. S7. It was obvious that after 48 h of incubation with 200.0 μg mL−1 of N-CDs, the cell viability was still over 85%. Thus the synthesized N-CDs are safe for in vitro applications. The blue excitation images of MG-63 incubated N-CDs solution (20.0 μg mL−1) clearly show strong green fluorescence (panels in Fig. 6 a) which revealed that a large amount of N-CDs penetrated into the cell membranes and it seems that they could keep their fluorescent natures in the cellular environment, while no photoluminescence was observed at nucleus. As expected, by the addition of 50.0 μM Fe3+ solution into the N-CDs-treated MG-63 cells, the intracellular fluorescence significantly decreased (panels in Fig. 6b). When the cells incubated with N-CDs-Fe3+, were further treated with increasing concentrations of ZA, a progressively fluorescence restoration in the cells was observed as depicted in bottom panels in Fig. 6 (Fig. 6 c). These observations supported the highly selective and switchable fluorescent process in living cells, so that the fluorescence images clearly show that by the addition of Fe3+, fluorescence emission is off and after addition of ZA due to the competitive reaction between the N-CDs and ZA drug on Fe3+, fluorescence emission is on. It can be concluded that as-synthesis N-CDs from date kernels can serve as promising candidates for chemical sensing and biological applications.

Herein, we attempted to develop an off-on switch system based on a simple, economical and eco-friendly hydrothermal synthesis of N-CDs using date kernel as starting material without further modification, which serves as a novel platform for ZA sensing in aqueous solution. The characterization of the N-CDs was completely investigated using TEM, Raman, XRD, FT-IR and EDX analysis. Additionally, the photophysical properties of as-prepared N-CDs as well as in vitro cytotoxic activity on tumor cells were investigated. The low cytotoxicity and excellent membrane permeability of the N-CDs demonstrated that it can act as an efficient fluorescent probe for bioimaging applications. It should also be mentioned that by using this novel nanoprobe, a much lower LOD (0.04 μmol L−1) than all previously reported methods for the determination of ZA was successfully achieved. As those described in this investigation, this study not only prepares a novel green source to synthesize N-doped CDs, but also demonstrates multifunctional applications of these N-CDs in both sensing and biological studies. Also the proposed probe can be used as a useful biological tool for routine monitoring of ZA.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Re-Supplemental file Re-Supplemental file

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.05.014.
